article.title: Isar Aerospace Targets Historic First Orbital Launch from European Soil with Spectrum's Second Flight
article.summary: German startup Isar Aerospace will attempt to send its Spectrum rocket to orbit from Norway's Andoya Spaceport on March 25, a milestone no vehicle has achieved from European territory.
article.body_markdown:
## A Second Chance at a Continental First

Isar Aerospace, the Munich-based rocket startup, is set to launch its Spectrum vehicle from Andoya Spaceport in northern Norway on March 25, with a launch window opening at 2000 GMT (9 p.m. local time). If the two-stage rocket reaches orbit, it will mark the first time any vehicle has done so from European soil, according to [a report from Space.com](https://www.space.com/space-exploration/launches-spacecraft/isar-aerospace-second-orbital-launch-attempt-andoya-spaceport).

The mission, named "Onward and Upward," follows a failed inaugural flight on March 30, 2025, when the first Spectrum rocket suffered an anomaly less than a minute after liftoff and crashed into the ocean near the pad. That setback made the upcoming attempt both a technical proving ground and a test of the company's ability to recover from failure on a compressed timeline.

## What Spectrum Carries This Time

Unlike the first test flight, which carried no operational payloads, the second mission will transport six passengers: five commercial and educational cubesats and one experiment designed to demonstrate new orbital technologies, [according to ESA](https://www.esa.int/Enabling_Support/Space_Transportation/Boost/Spectrum_s_qualifying_second_launch). The inclusion of student-built cubesats provides educational experiences for European university programs while also serving as a low-risk way to validate Spectrum's payload deployment systems.

Spectrum stands 28 meters tall with a 2-meter diameter and is powered by ten engines. The vehicle targets payloads of up to 1,000 kilograms to low Earth orbit, positioning it in the small-to-medium launch market that has seen growing demand from satellite constellation operators and institutional customers.

## ESA's Boost! Programme and the European Launcher Challenge

The flight is partially funded through ESA's Boost! programme, which co-finances commercial space transportation ventures to diversify Europe's launch options. ESA supported Isar Aerospace from its early days through the agency's Business Incubation Centre and continues to back the company's scaling of production facilities, [ESA noted](https://www.esa.int/Enabling_Support/Space_Transportation/Boost/Spectrum_s_qualifying_second_launch).

The mission also represents progress toward the European Launcher Challenge, an ESA initiative requiring participating companies to achieve orbital launches by 2027. ESA Director General Josef Aschbacher said the company "is poised to rise to a great challenge, one that will be instrumental in advancing a resilient and autonomous Europe in space," [according to ESA](https://www.esa.int/Enabling_Support/Space_Transportation/Boost/Spectrum_s_qualifying_second_launch).

Toni Tolker-Nielsen, ESA's Director of Space Transportation, offered a measured assessment: "Regardless of the outcome of this second flight, it will be another step to make European launch services market more diverse and resilient," [he said](https://www.esa.int/Enabling_Support/Space_Transportation/Boost/Spectrum_s_qualifying_second_launch).

## Why European Orbital Launch Has Proven Elusive

Despite decades of spaceflight heritage, Europe has never launched a rocket to orbit from its own territory. The continent's primary workhorse, the Ariane family of rockets, launches from the Guiana Space Centre in French Guiana, South America — a location chosen for its proximity to the equator, which provides a velocity boost for orbital insertions. Russia's Soyuz rockets, which previously launched from the same site under a cooperation agreement, were also non-European vehicles.

The push to establish orbital launch capability on European soil reflects a broader strategic calculation. Dependence on a single launch site in South America, combined with the post-2022 loss of access to Russian Soyuz rockets, exposed vulnerabilities in Europe's space access architecture. Multiple European startups, including Isar Aerospace, are now competing to fill that gap with smaller, commercially developed vehicles launched from sites in Norway, Sweden, and the United Kingdom, [as Space.com reported](https://www.space.com/space-exploration/launches-spacecraft/isar-aerospace-second-orbital-launch-attempt-andoya-spaceport).

## What Success or Failure Means

A successful orbital insertion would validate not only Spectrum's design but also the broader model of ESA investing in private European launch companies rather than relying solely on institutional programs. It would give Europe its first domestically launched orbital capability and establish Andoya as a viable polar-orbit launch site.

Failure, while not fatal to the program, would raise questions about the timeline for European commercial launch readiness. Isar Aerospace has not disclosed a manifest for subsequent flights, and the company's path to operational service depends heavily on demonstrating reliability early. The launch will be available to watch live, [according to Space.com](https://www.space.com/space-exploration/launches-spacecraft/isar-aerospace-second-orbital-launch-attempt-andoya-spaceport), offering real-time visibility into whether Europe finally joins the list of continents capable of reaching orbit from home.
